findings.description: fetch_events filters with created_at < end using a date-only ISO string, which excludes the entire end boundary day and violates the docstring contract that both boundary days are included.
DB-sourced display_name and action values are interpolated into HTML table rows without escaping, enabling stored XSS against admins who view the generated report.
title is substituted into the template's <title> and <h1> without HTML escaping, allowing XSS or markup breakage if the title is ever user-influenced.
top_users uses the same exclusive created_at < end bound, silently omitting the end day's activity from rankings, inconsistent with the inclusive-range semantics documented for fetch_events.
event_count excludes events on the end boundary day in both branches due to the created_at < end predicate with a date-only string.
limit is not validated; SQLite treats a negative LIMIT as no limit, so limit=-1 returns every user, and limit=0 silently returns none.
Building rows via string += in a loop is O(n^2) in the worst case; ''.join over a list is the linear pattern for large reports.
Date filtering assumes created_at is stored as ISO-8601 text; if it is stored as epoch numbers, SQLite's cross-type comparison (INTEGER < TEXT always) makes the predicates silently match nothing.
save_report writes to any caller-supplied out_path with no validation, enabling path traversal or arbitrary file overwrite if the path derives from untrusted input.
The caller-supplied title is logged verbatim, so embedded newlines could forge log entries (log injection).
The HTML template declares no <meta charset>, so the UTF-8 file written by save_report may render non-ASCII display names garbled when the browser guesses the encoding.
fetchall materializes the full event range in memory; very large date ranges could be expensive compared to iterating the cursor.
sqlite3.connect silently creates an empty database file when db_path does not exist, masking misconfiguration behind a later 'no such table' error and leaving a stray file.
Every public method opens and closes a fresh SQLite connection, adding overhead when one report flow calls several methods back to back.
ORDER BY events DESC has no tie-breaker, so users with equal counts are returned in nondeterministic order across runs.
No validation that start <= end; reversed arguments silently yield an empty result instead of an error.
The column alias 'events' shadows the table name 'events' in the same query, which is confusing to read.
The two event_count branches duplicate a nearly identical query differing only by one predicate; the SQL could be built conditionally to avoid drift.
border="1" is an obsolete HTML presentational attribute; styling belongs in CSS.
The class and most public methods lack docstrings; only fetch_events documents its contract.
